text: In an INOR-8 hydrofluorination vessel, rejected fully enriched zirconium-uranium alloy or dummy Zircaloy-2 fuel elements are dissolved at $500 - 600^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ in NaF-LiF-ZrF $_4$ salts using an excess of anhydrous HF gas.

In the fluorinator, which is constructed of L nickel and operated at $500^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , elemental fluorine converts $\mathrm{UF_4}$ to $\mathrm{UF_6}$ product which is decontaminated and collected in cold traps.
Figure 1 shows a simplified flowsheet of the volatility process.

Three fluorinators have been used to date in the pilot plant, and all three have sustained a maximum corrosion attack of approximately 1 mil/hr, based on the fluorine exposure times. $^{1,2}$ Efforts to reduce the corrosive attack on fluorination vessels have included minimizing the operating temperature, careful exclusion of sulfur-containing compounds, and the search for a more resistant construction material.
Accordingly, in the first two pilot plant fluorinators, Mark I and II, corrosion tests were performed using many potential container materials, mostly nickel-base alloys. $^{3}$
To date, 24 specimens have been exposed in the Mark III fluorinator.
Descriptions of the Group I and II test specimens, their location, and chemical compositions are shown in Tables 1 and 2.
The control specimens consisted of 3-in. lengths of $\frac{1}{4}$ -in.-diam L nickel rod joined together with $\frac{1}{4}$ -in. deposits of INCO-61 weld metal.

The control specimen from Group I and one of the controls from Group II were fluorine conditioned for 3.2 hr at $600 - 645^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ before testing to ascertain the effect of conditioning on corrosion.
Subsequently, the high-purity nickel was cast into a 1-in.-diam bar, cold swaged and cold drawn down to an 0.250-in. rod, and annealed at $1500^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .
Figure 4 shows a polar view of the fluorination chamber and the plan layout of the corrosion nozzles.

Total corrosion noted for the specimens is detailed in Tables 8 and 9.
In Group II, intergranular attack was found mainly in the L nickel and 95 Ni-5 Co specimens.
For the unconditioned L nickel specimen, intergranular attack to a depth of 6 mils was noted.

As Table 13 indicates, during corrosion tests the L nickel specimens were exposed in $\mathrm{NaF - ZrF_4}$ salts (usually with $\mathrm{UF_4}$ present), while the Group I/Group II tests were exposed to a LiF-NaF-ZrF4 mixture.

In Group II, one of the L nickel-INCO 61 specimens was fluorine conditioned $3.2\mathrm{hr}$ at $600 - 645^{\circ}C$ before corrosion testing.
On the other hand, conditioned INCO-61 weld metal exposed in the vapor and salt regions exhibited greater attack than did unconditioned INCO 61.
The titanium present in INCO 61 possibly modifies and makes the $\mathrm{NiF}_2$ film less stable since titanium fluorides are highly volatile.

The combination of operating temperatures around $550^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ with lithium fluoride present in the salt baths produces about the same bulk metal losses on nickel and nickel-base alloys as was found by operating at $650^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ without lithium in the salt baths.

Bulk metal losses on L nickel and the nickel-binary alloys containing manganese, iron, cobalt, or aluminum are about the same during fluorination at $550^{\circ}C$ with lithium present in the fluoride salt baths.
These reported tests showed that serious intergranular attack of a 95 Ni-5 Co specimen occurred.
On the other hand, this test series and previous series showed good resistance to this form of corrosion by nickel containing 10 and 20 wt % Co.
Sections from 98 Ni-2 Mn Specimen 32 from Group I Volatility Pilot Plant Fluorinator Test Specimens.
